title: Chemistry
description: Chemistry from scratch for complete beginners (in progress): what stuff is made of and how it changes — matter and atoms, the periodic table, bonding and molecules, the mole, reactions, states of matter, gases, solutions, acids and bases, energy and rates, carbon and the chemistry of life. Beginner-first readings, a diagram in nearly every lesson, MCQ practice, tiered easy to hard.

subtopics.title: What Chemistry Is: Matter & the Particle View

lessons.title: What Chemistry Is, and Why It's Everywhere
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Have you ever fried an egg, watched a nail go rusty, or charged a phone? Then you have already met chemistry. In fact, cooking is just chemistry you can eat.

**Build.** **Chemistry** is the study of **matter** — what stuff is made of and how it changes into other stuff. So what is matter? **Matter** is anything that has **mass** (a measure of how much stuff is there) and takes up **space** (it has size, a volume you could in principle measure). That covers an enormous amount: you, the air around you, a glass of water, a rock, a slice of bread. If it is 'stuff,' it is matter, and chemistry is interested in it.

Because matter is everywhere, chemistry is everywhere too. It is the bubbling when you bake, the cleaning power of soap lifting grease off a plate, the way your body digests food and turns it into energy, the reaction inside a battery that lights up a flashlight, and the slow browning of a cut apple. None of this requires a lab coat — it is happening in your kitchen and your body right now.

A chemist's basic move is simple: take some matter, do something to it (heat it, mix it, leave it in the air), and carefully watch what changes. That habit of looking closely is the heart of the subject.

**Recap.** Chemistry is the study of matter — what things are made of and how they change. Matter is anything with mass that takes up space, so chemistry shows up everywhere, from cooking to rust to batteries.

items.prompt: Chemistry is best described as the study of:
choices: Only liquids kept inside a laboratory
Matter — what stuff is made of and how it changes into other stuff
Only living plants and animals
Numbers and equations, with no real-world stuff involved
items.explanation: Chemistry studies matter: what things are made of and how they change. It is not limited to lab liquids (choice 1) or to living things (choice 3), and it is about real physical stuff, not just math on paper (choice 4).

items.prompt: In chemistry, 'matter' means:
choices: Anything that has mass and takes up space
Only things you can pour, like water
Only things heavier than a rock
Anything that gives off light
items.explanation: Matter is anything with mass that takes up space — solids, liquids, and gases all count. It is not limited to pourable liquids (choice 2) or to heavy things (choice 3), and giving off light (choice 4) is not part of the definition.

items.prompt: Which of these is an example of matter?
choices: The air in a balloon
A loud sound
The light from a lamp
The idea of happiness
items.explanation: Air has mass and takes up space (it fills the balloon), so it is matter. A sound (choice 2) and light (choice 3) carry energy but are not stuff with mass and volume, and an idea (choice 4) is not physical at all.

items.prompt: Why is it fair to say 'cooking is chemistry you can eat'?
choices: Because recipes are written by professional chemists
Because cooking takes matter (food) and changes it into new, different stuff
Because food contains no matter until it is cooked
Because only food that is cooked in a laboratory counts as chemistry
items.explanation: Cooking takes matter and changes it — exactly what chemistry studies. Recipes are not written by chemists as a rule (choice 1), raw food is already matter (choice 3), and chemistry is not limited to laboratories (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday event is an example of chemistry at work?
choices: A ball rolling down a hill at steady speed
A book sitting still on a shelf
A shadow growing longer in the evening
A nail slowly turning rusty in the rain
items.explanation: Rust forms when iron in the nail changes into a new substance — a chemical change, which is chemistry. A rolling ball (choice 1) and a longer shadow (choice 3) are about motion and light, and a still book (choice 2) is not changing into new stuff.

items.prompt: 'Mass' is best understood as:
choices: How brightly something glows
The color of an object
How loud something is
A measure of how much stuff something contains
items.explanation: Mass measures how much stuff (matter) is there. Brightness (choice 1), color (choice 2), and loudness (choice 3) are other properties and do not tell you the amount of matter.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'Chemistry only happens in a lab with special equipment.' The best reply is:
choices: True — nothing chemical happens outside a lab
False — chemistry is happening all around us, like digestion, rust, and batteries
True — cooking and cleaning have nothing to do with chemistry
False — but only because labs are too expensive to build
items.explanation: Chemistry is everywhere matter changes — in your body, in rusting metal, in a battery. So the claim is false. Choices 1 and 3 wrongly restrict chemistry to labs, and choice 4 gives an irrelevant reason instead of the real one.

items.prompt: What is a chemist's most basic working habit?
choices: Memorizing long lists without ever looking at anything
Doing something to matter and then carefully observing what changes
Avoiding any kind of change so nothing ever happens
Guessing the answer without testing it
items.explanation: Chemistry is built on doing something to matter (heating, mixing, waiting) and watching closely. Pure memorizing (choice 1) and guessing (choice 4) skip the observation, and avoiding all change (choice 3) means there is nothing to study.

lessons.title: The Particle View: Everything Is Tiny Moving Bits
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Someone opens a bottle of perfume across the room, and a moment later you smell it. Nothing visibly flew toward you — so how did the scent get to your nose?

**Build.** The answer is the single biggest idea in chemistry: the **particle view**. All matter is made of incredibly tiny **particles** — think of them as the smallest bits of stuff, far too small to see even with an ordinary microscope. (Many of these particles are **atoms** or small groups of atoms; for now, just picture tiny bits.) Two facts make this idea powerful. First, these particles are unimaginably small. Second, they are **always moving** — never truly still.

How they move depends on the state of the matter. In a **solid**, the particles are packed tightly and only **jiggle** in place. In a **liquid**, they are looser and **flow** past one another. In a **gas**, they are far apart and **zoom** around quickly. Same kind of particle — just different spacing and speed.

This one picture explains so much. The perfume smell reaches you because scent particles **drift** through the air, mixing with the air particles until some reach your nose. Stir sugar into tea and it seems to 'disappear' — really its particles **spread out** evenly among the water particles, too scattered to see. Heat an ice cube and its particles **speed up** until they break loose and flow as water. You cannot see any of this, yet it quietly explains almost everything ahead.

**Recap.** All matter is made of tiny particles in constant motion: tightly jiggling in solids, flowing in liquids, zooming in gases. This particle view explains smells spreading, sugar dissolving, and ice melting.

items.prompt: The 'particle view' says that all matter is made of:
choices: One smooth, solid, unbroken material with no gaps
Tiny particles that are always moving
Particles that exist only in gases, not in solids
Nothing — matter is empty space
items.explanation: The particle view holds that all matter is built from tiny, constantly moving particles. Matter is not a smooth continuous material (choice 1) — that is the common misconception this idea replaces. Solids have particles too (choice 3), and matter is real stuff, not nothing (choice 4).

items.prompt: You smell perfume from across the room. The particle view explains this by saying:
choices: Smell travels as a beam of light to your eyes
The perfume bottle pushes a solid block of scent across the floor
The perfume particles drift through the air until some reach your nose
Nothing actually moves; your brain invents the smell
items.explanation: Scent particles spread and drift through the air, mixing among the air particles until they reach you. Smell is not light (choice 1), it is not a solid block sliding over (choice 2), and real particles do reach your nose (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a SOLID, the particles mostly:
choices: Are far apart and zoom around quickly
Are packed tightly and only jiggle in place
Have disappeared, leaving empty space
Flow freely and pour like a liquid
items.explanation: Solid particles are packed close and vibrate (jiggle) in fixed spots, which is why solids hold their shape. Far apart and fast describes a gas (choice 1), pouring describes a liquid (choice 4), and the particles do not vanish (choice 3).

items.prompt: When you stir sugar into tea and it seems to 'disappear,' what has really happened to the sugar?
choices: Its particles were destroyed and no longer exist
It turned into water
It floated away into the air as a gas
Its particles spread out evenly among the water particles, too scattered to see
items.explanation: Dissolving spreads the sugar particles evenly among the water particles, so they are still there but too scattered to see (the tea even tastes sweet). The sugar is not destroyed (choice 1), does not become water (choice 2), and does not boil off into the air (choice 3).

items.prompt: How do the particles in a GAS behave compared with those in a solid or liquid?
choices: They are locked in place and cannot move
They are far apart and zoom around quickly
They are packed even tighter than in a solid
They stop moving once the gas fills a room
items.explanation: Gas particles are spread far apart and move fast, which lets a gas spread to fill its container. They are not locked in place (choice 1), they are much farther apart than in a solid (choice 3), and they keep moving constantly (choice 4).

items.prompt: What happens to the particles in an ice cube as it melts into water?
choices: They speed up until they break loose and flow past one another
They slow down and stop moving
They turn into completely different particles
They shrink to half their size
items.explanation: Adding heat makes the particles move faster until they break free of their fixed spots and flow as a liquid. They speed up, not slow down (choice 2); the same particles remain (choice 3); and individual particles do not change size (choice 4).

items.prompt: A beginner pictures water as a smooth, gap-free material rather than as particles. According to the particle view, this picture is:
choices: Correct — matter really is smooth and continuous with no tiny bits
A common misconception — matter is actually made of separate tiny particles
Correct only for water but wrong for everything else
Impossible to decide either way
items.explanation: The whole point of the particle view is that matter is not smooth and continuous — it is made of separate tiny particles. So this is a classic misconception (choice 1 is the error). It applies to all matter, not just water (choice 3), and chemistry has strong evidence for it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is the particle view called the big foundational idea of chemistry?
choices: Because it only matters for gases and nothing else
Because this one picture explains so many everyday things, like smells spreading and sugar dissolving
Because it lets you see individual particles with your bare eyes
Because it proves matter has no mass
items.explanation: The particle view is foundational because the same simple picture explains a huge range of observations — melting, smells drifting, dissolving, and more. It applies to all states, not just gases (choice 1); particles are too small to see directly (choice 3); and matter does have mass (choice 4).

lessons.title: Pure Substances vs Mixtures
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Pour yourself a glass of pure water and a glass of salt water. They look the same, but to a chemist they are very different kinds of thing. Why?

**Build.** Chemists sort matter into two groups. A **pure substance** is made of just **one kind of stuff** — every particle in it is the same type. Pure water is one kind of stuff; a bar of pure gold is one kind of stuff. There is nothing else mixed in.

A **mixture** is **two or more kinds of stuff mixed together**. A salad is lettuce, tomato, and dressing all in one bowl. The air you breathe is a mixture of several gases. Salt water is salt mixed into water. In a mixture, each ingredient keeps being itself — the salt is still salt and the water is still water, just blended.

Here is a handy clue: because the parts of a mixture keep their own identity, a mixture can often be **separated** back into its ingredients. Let salt water sit in the sun and the water drifts off as a gas, leaving the salt behind. You could pick the tomatoes out of a salad. A pure substance, made of only one kind of stuff, cannot be split this way — there is nothing else in there to separate.

A common trap is to call salt water 'pure' because it looks clean and clear. Looks do not decide it: salt water contains two different kinds of stuff, so it is a mixture, not a pure substance.

**Recap.** A pure substance is one kind of stuff (pure water, pure gold); a mixture is two or more kinds mixed together (salad, air, salt water) and can often be separated again. Looking clear does not make something pure.

items.prompt: A 'pure substance' is matter that is made of:
choices: Two or more kinds of stuff blended together
Just one kind of stuff
Only liquids, never solids
Anything as long as it looks clean
items.explanation: A pure substance contains only one kind of stuff, with every particle the same type. Two or more kinds blended is a mixture (choice 1). Pure substances can be solid, liquid, or gas (choice 3), and looking clean does not decide purity (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which of these is a MIXTURE?
choices: A bar of pure gold
A sample of pure water
A bowl of salad
A single kind of particle by itself
items.explanation: A salad is several different foods combined, so it is a mixture. Pure gold (choice 1) and pure water (choice 2) are each one kind of stuff, and a single kind of particle (choice 4) is the opposite of a mixture.

items.prompt: Is the air you breathe a pure substance or a mixture?
choices: A pure substance, because it is invisible
A mixture, because it is several different gases together
A pure substance, because it has no mass
Neither, because gases are not matter
items.explanation: Air is several gases (such as nitrogen and oxygen) mixed together, so it is a mixture. Being invisible (choice 1) does not make it pure, air does have mass (choice 3), and gases are matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: Someone argues that salt water is a pure substance because it looks perfectly clear. The best response is:
choices: Agree — if it looks clear, it must be pure
Disagree — salt water contains two kinds of stuff (salt and water), so it is a mixture
Agree — water always makes things pure
Disagree — but only because salt water is cloudy
items.explanation: Looking clear does not make something pure; salt water has two kinds of stuff and is a mixture. Choices 1 and 3 fall for the 'clear means pure' trap, and choice 4 gives a wrong reason since salt water is actually clear.

items.prompt: Why can a mixture often be separated back into its ingredients?
choices: Because each ingredient keeps being itself within the mixture
Because mixtures contain only one kind of stuff
Because separating always destroys the ingredients
Because mixtures are not really matter
items.explanation: In a mixture, the parts keep their own identity (salt stays salt, water stays water), so they can be pulled apart again. A mixture has more than one kind of stuff (choice 2), separating recovers the parts rather than destroying them (choice 3), and mixtures are matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: If you leave salt water sitting in the sun until the water drifts away as a gas, what is left behind?
choices: Nothing at all
The salt
Pure gold
A brand-new substance that was never there before
items.explanation: The water leaves as a gas and the salt stays behind, showing salt water was a mixture you can separate. Something remains, so not nothing (choice 1); it is salt, not gold (choice 3); and no new substance is created — the salt was there all along (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the particle picture, how does a pure substance look different from a mixture?
choices: A pure substance has all-same particles; a mixture has different kinds mixed together
A pure substance has different kinds of particles; a mixture has all-same particles
Both look exactly the same at the particle level
Neither one is made of particles
items.explanation: Picture it as circles: a pure substance is all identical circles, while a mixture has different shapes mixed in. Choice 2 reverses this, choice 3 ignores the difference, and choice 4 forgets that all matter is made of particles.

items.prompt: Which list contains ONLY pure substances?
choices: Salad, air, salt water
Pure gold, a bowl of cereal with milk
Air, lemonade
Pure water, pure gold
items.explanation: Pure water and pure gold are each a single kind of stuff. Salad, air, salt water, cereal-with-milk, and lemonade are all mixtures of several things, so choices 1, 2, and 3 each include at least one mixture.

lessons.title: Physical vs Chemical Changes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Melt a stick of butter in a warm pan and it turns to liquid. Crack an egg into that same pan and it sets into a firm white. Both 'changed' — but only one became something genuinely new. Spotting the difference is a key chemistry skill.

**Build.** Chemists split changes into two kinds. A **physical change** does **not** make a new substance — the stuff is the same, just in a different form. Melting ice into water is physical: it is still water, just liquid now. Dissolving sugar in tea is physical: the sugar particles only spread out. Cutting a sheet of paper is physical: smaller pieces, but still paper. A useful sign of a physical change is that you can often **reverse** it — freeze the water back into ice, and you are right where you started.

A **chemical change** is different: it makes a **brand-new substance** with new properties. Burning wood turns it into ash and smoke — you cannot un-burn it back into a log. Iron rusting turns shiny metal into crumbly orange rust. Baking a cake turns runny batter into something you could never separate back into flour and eggs. A battery works by chemical change. Chemical changes are usually **hard to reverse**, because the original stuff is genuinely gone.

The big question to ask is simply: **did I end up with new stuff, or the same stuff in a new form?** New stuff means a chemical change. Same stuff, new form, means a physical change. A common trap is thinking that dissolving sugar is chemical — it is not, because no new substance forms; the sugar is still sugar.

**Recap.** A physical change keeps the same substance in a new form (melting, dissolving, cutting) and is often reversible. A chemical change makes a new substance (burning, rusting, baking) and is usually hard to reverse. Ask: new stuff, or same stuff?

items.prompt: The key difference between a physical change and a chemical change is:
choices: A physical change makes a new substance; a chemical change does not
Both always make brand-new substances
A chemical change makes a new substance; a physical change does not
Neither one ever changes matter in any way
items.explanation: A chemical change produces new stuff with new properties, while a physical change leaves the same substance in a new form. Choice 1 reverses the definitions — a common mistake. Physical changes do not make new substances (choice 2), and both clearly change matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: Melting an ice cube into water is which kind of change?
choices: A chemical change, because the ice is destroyed
A physical change, because it is still water, just in a new form
A chemical change, because a new substance forms
Neither, because nothing happens at all
items.explanation: Melting is physical: solid water becomes liquid water, the same substance in a different form, and you can freeze it back. No new substance forms (choices 1 and 3 are the misconception), and a real change does occur (choice 4).

items.prompt: Burning a sheet of paper into ash and smoke is which kind of change?
choices: A physical change, because the paper is just in a new form
A chemical change, because new substances (ash and smoke) are formed
A physical change, because you can easily turn the ash back into paper
Neither, because burning is not a change
items.explanation: Burning makes brand-new substances — ash and smoke — so it is a chemical change, and you cannot un-burn it. It is not merely a new form of paper (choice 1), the ash cannot be turned back into paper (choice 3), and burning is certainly a change (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says dissolving sugar in water is a chemical change. Why is that wrong?
choices: Because no new substance forms — the sugar is still sugar, just spread out
Because dissolving destroys the sugar forever
Because dissolving turns the sugar into salt
Because dissolving only works with chemical changes
items.explanation: Dissolving is physical: the sugar particles simply spread among the water, and the sugar is still sugar (the water tastes sweet). No new substance appears, so calling it chemical is the classic error. The sugar is not destroyed (choice 2) and does not become salt (choice 3); choice 4 misstates what dissolving is.

items.prompt: Frying an egg until it sets is a chemical change because:
choices: The egg is simply changing shape but stays the same stuff
The runny egg turns into a new, firm substance you cannot turn back
Frying never changes any food
The egg is only melting like butter
items.explanation: Cooking the egg creates a new substance — the firm cooked white — that cannot be returned to a raw egg, so it is chemical. It is not just a shape change (choice 1), frying does change food (choice 3), and the egg is not melting (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which group lists only PHYSICAL changes?
choices: Burning wood, iron rusting, baking a cake
Frying an egg, rusting a nail, burning toast
Baking bread, a battery working, rust forming
Melting ice, dissolving sugar, cutting paper
items.explanation: Melting, dissolving, and cutting all keep the same substance in a new form, so all are physical. The other groups contain burning, rusting, and baking, which make new substances and are therefore chemical changes.

items.prompt: Which single question best tells a physical change from a chemical change?
choices: Did the change happen quickly or slowly?
Did I end up with new stuff, or the same stuff in a new form?
Did the change happen in a kitchen or a lab?
Was the object large or small to begin with?
items.explanation: The deciding question is whether a new substance formed (chemical) or the same substance just took a new form (physical). Speed (choice 1), location (choice 3), and size (choice 4) do not determine which kind of change it is.

items.prompt: Ice melting to water and freezing back to ice can be repeated over and over. Burning toast cannot be undone. This shows that:
choices: Physical changes are often reversible, while chemical changes are usually hard to reverse
Chemical changes are always easy to reverse
Physical changes can never be reversed
Reversibility has nothing to do with the type of change
items.explanation: Because a physical change keeps the same substance, it can often be reversed; a chemical change makes new stuff and is usually hard to reverse. Chemical changes are not easy to reverse (choice 2), physical changes often can be reversed (choice 3), and reversibility is a real clue (choice 4).

lessons.title: Signs of a Chemical Change, and the Observer's Mindset
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You mix two clear liquids and suddenly the cup fizzes, warms up, and turns cloudy. Something new just formed — but how would you know for sure? Chemists watch for a few telltale signs.

**Build.** A **chemical change** makes a new substance, and it usually announces itself with one or more clues. The main signs to watch for are: a **color change** (an apple slice browning, a leaf turning red); a **gas given off**, often seen as **bubbles** (vinegar and baking soda fizzing); **heat or light released** (a flame, hand-warmers getting hot); and a **solid forming** out of liquids, which chemists call a **precipitate** (a cloudy solid appearing when two clear liquids meet). On top of these, chemical changes are usually **hard to reverse** — you cannot easily get the original stuff back.

Notice that a physical change shows **none** of these new-substance signs. Melting ice or dissolving sugar produces no new color, no gas, no released heat, and no precipitate — just the same stuff in a new form. So the signs are a practical checklist: spot one, and a chemical change has likely happened.

All of this rests on one habit: **careful observation**. Chemistry is learned by paying close attention — noting what you see, smell, and feel changing, ideally writing it down. And one plain rule of the mindset: treat every chemical with respect and follow safe handling, because some substances can harm you even when a change looks calm.

**Recap.** Signs of a chemical change are a color change, a gas or bubbles, heat or light given off, and a solid (precipitate) forming — and it is usually hard to reverse. Physical changes show none of these. Above all, observe carefully and handle chemicals safely.

items.prompt: Which of these is a sign that a CHEMICAL change has happened?
choices: The ingredients keep exactly the same color, smell, and form
Bubbles of a gas are given off
An ice cube simply gets smaller as it melts
Sugar quietly spreads through tea with no other change
items.explanation: Producing a gas (bubbles) is a classic sign of a chemical change. Staying exactly the same (choice 1) signals no chemical change, and melting ice (choice 3) and dissolving sugar (choice 4) are physical changes with none of the telltale signs.

items.prompt: Which list gives common signs of a chemical change?
choices: Getting colder only, with no other change
Changing shape, then easily changing back
Color change, gas or bubbles, heat or light, a solid forming
Looking clear, looking clean, looking shiny
items.explanation: The standard signs are a color change, gas/bubbles, heat or light given off, and a solid (precipitate) forming. Choice 2 describes an easily reversed physical change, and choices 1 and 4 are not the recognized signs of new-substance formation.

items.prompt: In chemistry, a 'precipitate' is:
choices: A gas that escapes into the air
A solid that forms, often when two liquids are mixed
The heat given off during a reaction
A color that fades away to nothing
items.explanation: A precipitate is a solid that appears — for example, a cloudy solid forming when two clear liquids are combined — and it signals a chemical change. It is not a gas (choice 1), not heat (choice 3), and not a fading color (choice 4).

items.prompt: You melt some ice and watch closely. Which is true about the signs of a chemical change?
choices: You see all four signs, so melting is chemical
You see none of the signs, which fits melting being a physical change
You see bubbles of a new gas, so melting is chemical
You see a precipitate form, so melting is chemical
items.explanation: Melting ice produces no new color, no gas, no released heat, and no precipitate — it shows none of the signs because it is a physical change. Claiming you see those signs (choices 1, 3, 4) misreads what happens when ice simply turns to water.

items.prompt: Besides the visible signs, what else is usually true of a chemical change?
choices: It can always be reversed in a single easy step
It never gives off heat or light
It leaves the substance completely unchanged
It is usually hard to reverse, because the original stuff is genuinely gone
items.explanation: Because a chemical change makes new substances, the original is gone and it is usually hard to undo. It is not easily reversible (choice 1), it often does give off heat or light (choice 2), and the substance is changed, not left the same (choice 3).

items.prompt: Vinegar poured on baking soda fizzes vigorously. The fizzing is a sign of a chemical change because it shows:
choices: A gas is being produced
The mixture is simply getting colder with no new stuff
The baking soda is only changing shape
Nothing new is happening
items.explanation: The fizz is a gas being given off — one of the clearest signs that a new substance formed. It is not a mere temperature drop with no new stuff (choice 2) or a shape change (choice 3), and something new is clearly happening (choice 4).

items.prompt: How is chemistry mainly learned, according to the observer's mindset?
choices: By never looking at anything and only guessing
By careful observation — noting what you see, smell, and feel changing
By assuming nothing ever changes
By memorizing colors without watching any change
items.explanation: Chemistry is learned by observing closely and noting changes you see, smell, and feel. Guessing without looking (choice 1), assuming nothing changes (choice 3), and rote memorizing with no observation (choice 4) all skip the careful watching at the heart of the subject.

items.prompt: What is the sensible safety attitude when working with chemicals?
choices: Treat every chemical with respect and follow safe handling
Assume anything that looks calm is completely harmless
Taste unknown substances to identify them
Ignore safety because chemistry is always gentle
items.explanation: Respect chemicals and handle them safely, since some can harm you even when a change looks calm. Assuming calm-looking substances are harmless (choice 2), tasting unknowns (choice 3), and ignoring safety (choice 4) are all unsafe.
